does: This paper introduces LOCA, a method for providing local causal explanations of why specific jailbreak attacks succeed against safety-trained LLMs. Rather than offering global explanations that describe all jailbreaks as reducing harmfulness or strengthening refusal, LOCA identifies which specific intermediate concepts are causally altered for a particular jailbreak-request pair. The method isolates minimal sets of causal factors that explain individual jailbreak successes, enabling fine-grained understanding of attack mechanisms that may differ across jailbreak strategies and harmful request categories. The authors move beyond treating jailbreaks as a monolithic phenomenon, instead providing targeted explanations for why particular attacks work in particular contexts.
connects: This work builds on prior research using causal intervention methods to understand LLM vulnerabilities by examining intermediate representations. Earlier studies identified directions in activation space encoding concepts like harmfulness and refusal, proposing global mechanistic explanations for jailbreak susceptibility. LOCA extends this line by recognizing limitations of one-size-fits-all explanations—that different jailbreak strategies may operate through distinct causal pathways and that effectiveness varies across request categories like violence versus cyberattacks. The paper responds to the need for more granular mechanistic interpretability, drawing on techniques from causal inference to localize explanations rather than generalizing across all attacks. This connects to broader efforts in interpretability research seeking to understand model behavior through intervention and causal analysis.
enables: By providing local causal explanations of jailbreak success, this work enables more targeted defenses tailored to specific attack vectors and request types rather than single broad mitigations. Understanding which intermediate concepts causally drive individual jailbreaks could inform more precise safety interventions. The methodology also establishes a foundation for adversarial red-teaming by identifying minimal causal factors sufficient for attack success, potentially guiding discovery of new vulnerabilities. Future work could leverage these explanations to build defenses robust to attacks operating through different causal pathways, or to develop more sophisticated safety training that accounts for multiple failure modes. The approach scales to increasingly autonomous and capable frontier models where understanding specific vulnerability mechanisms becomes critical for high-stakes deployment.
